title: Spermacoce arenicola
taxonomy: Spermacoce arenicola is classified within the family Rubiaceae, commonly known as the coffee or madder family. This family comprises approximately 13,200 species, making it one of the largest families in the plant kingdom. Spermacoce arenicola, along with other members of the Rubiaceae family, exhibits distinct characteristics such as opposite leaves and small, tubular flowers. The genus Spermacoce itself contains around 200 species, primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions. Spermacoce arenicola is further categorized into the order Gentianales, which includes other flowering plant families like Apocynaceae and Loganiaceae.

side_effects: Spermacoce arenicola, like many plants in the same genus, can be toxic to humans and animals if ingested. Ingesting parts of the plant can cause a range of side effects, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. The toxic compounds found in Spermacoce arenicola may also cause cardiac problems and other systemic issues. There is limited information available on the long-term effects of exposure to this plant, but it is generally recommended to avoid contact with its sap and leaves. intro: Spermacoce arenicola, also known as coastal false buttonweed, is a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. Traditionally, Spermacoce arenicola has been used for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects that may aid in the treatment of respiratory issues, wounds, and infections. The bioactive constituents of Spermacoce arenicola include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Spermacoce arenicola is often used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are commonly used to treat coughs, colds, and other respiratory ailments.
